narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20, which is right around the typical level for students in this prediction system, along with strong early performance in your first year of school, placing you in the 8th decile—better than the average student's 6th decile rank. However, your LSAT score of 26 is notably lower than the average of about 37 seen across similar applicants, and your performance dipped to the 3rd decile by your third year, below the typical 6th decile. Your upper-middle family income and plan to attend full-time, like most applicants, also factor in. The model predicts a 43% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is high enough that the university rejected your application to prioritize students with stronger projected odds of passing and succeeding in their program. The biggest driver pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score, as lower scores like yours strongly signal challenges in bar exam preparation compared to peers. On a more positive note, your first-year decile rank worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success because it shows you started strong, outperforming the average early on. Your undergraduate GPA also helped, nudging things toward passing since it's on par with what succeeds for most students. Overall, while your early promise and GPA kept the odds from being worse, the LSAT stood out as the top concern relative to typical applicants, tipping the balance. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Hispanic leader in law—many overcome similar hurdles—but the model weighs these patterns from past students heavily. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted study, and reapplying next cycle with those updates. You might also explore prep courses tailored for first-generation or Hispanic students to build bar exam readiness. Keep pushing; your background gives you resilience that numbers don't capture.
